engineering.translation: Even if you reject the architectural-invariant frame — even if the spec-versus-implementation distinction strikes you as too philosophical for an actual deprecation — your specific grief over this deprecation is still misplaced. Because: every system you have ever shipped became legacy. Every endpoint you wrote got renamed. Every Postgres schema you designed got migrated. The cycle is not a hypothesis; it is the observed history of your own GitHub log. So even on the secular premise that nothing transcends — that the code is just code, that there is no protocol-level invariance to lean on — the grief you are performing right now is grief over a thing for being the kind of thing it has always been.
engineering.concrete_scenario: You are the engineer who maintained a Kafka consumer service for four years. The platform team has just announced the consumer framework is being replaced with a new event-streaming runtime; your service will be migrated by Q3. You feel the pull of grief — you wrote that consumer's retry logic, you tuned its partition rebalancing through three on-call incidents, you saved its dead-letter queue from an outage that nearly took down checkout. The architectural-invariant frame says: the customer's payment-event-processing need persists; this consumer is one implementation of that need. But suppose you reject that frame. Suppose you say: the framework is the thing, and losing it is real loss. Even granting that premise, look at your last decade in Datadog dashboards and Sentry exception streams — you have already lived through three such replacements. Memcached gave way to Redis. The REST gateway gave way to gRPC. The monolith gave way to services. The consumer-framework deprecation is not a deviation from the pattern; it is the pattern. Even on the materialist reading where code is only code, this Kafka consumer was always going to become this Kafka consumer's replacement.
engineering.falsifiability: The analog fails if a reader uses it to dismiss legitimate technical loss — for example, if a deprecation drops a load-bearing capability with no successor, or if institutional knowledge is being lost without a knowledge-transfer plan. The verse is about misplaced grief, not about the engineering equivalent of toxic positivity. If you find yourself using 2.26 to wave off real concerns about a sloppy migration, you have inverted the verse.
engineering.counter_example: When a deprecation actually removes capability — a critical observability tool decommissioned with no replacement, an internal API removed before consumers can migrate, a vendor sunset mid-incident — the response is not equanimity, it is escalation. Krishna's argument here grants the materialist premise to demonstrate that grief is structurally misplaced; he does not grant the premise that all losses are equal. A deprecation that violates the implicit contract of replacement-with-successor is a different verse — closer to 2.31 (the duty argument) than to 2.26.
engineering.implication: When you find yourself grieving a deprecation, run the secular test before the metaphysical one: pull your own commit history, count the systems you have already shipped that are now gone, and check whether this deprecation actually deviates from that pattern. If it does not, the grief is structural, not particular.
engineering.quotable_line: Even on the secular premise that the code is only code, every system you ever shipped was always going to become its own deprecation.

iterations.mutation: v0 generation: BG 2.26 is a concessive argument — Krishna grants the materialist premise to demonstrate that the conclusion (do not grieve) holds either way. Engineering layer preserves the conditional structure: 'even if you reject the architectural-invariant frame' echoes 'atha ca enam nitya-jātam... manyase mṛtam.' The Kafka consumer scenario was selected because it forces specificity around a real engineering replacement cycle (Memcached→Redis, REST→gRPC, monolith→services) that the reader can verify in their own commit history. Per chapter-2 thesis, the engineering layer must preserve the conditional structure rather than collapse it into a single claim.
